questions.prompt: Listen to the voicemail. What time does Sunday's ride now start?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Woman] Hi, it's Amara from the cycling club. Just a quick note about Sunday's ride. We were going to meet at nine o'clock, but the café by the bridge now opens early, so we'll set off at half past eight instead. We should still be back by twelve. See you there!

questions.prompt: Listen to the announcement. Where should visitors leave their bicycles?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Man] Good morning, and welcome to the park festival. A few quick points. The bicycle repair tent is beside the lake, and the food stalls are just inside the main gate. Please do not leave your bicycle at the gate. There are racks behind the visitor centre, and you should lock your bicycle there. Thank you.

questions.prompt: Listen to the conversation in a shop. What does the woman buy?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Woman] Excuse me, do you sell puncture repair kits? [Man] We've sold out of them today, I'm afraid. We do have inner tubes and tyre levers, though. [Woman] Hmm. I need to fix my back wheel this evening. I'll take an inner tube, then, please. [Man] Certainly. That's four pounds.

questions.prompt: Listen to the message. What does the speaker say members should bring?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Man] Hi, it's Tariq. Just a reminder about tomorrow's ride to the coast. The forecast says it will be cold but dry, so you won't need a raincoat, and there's no need for an umbrella either. Do bring gloves, though — your hands get very cold on the way down the hill.

questions.prompt: Listen to the voicemail. Why has tomorrow's group ride been cancelled?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Woman] Hi Marcus, it's Nina from the club. Bad news about tomorrow's ride, I'm afraid. The weather actually looks fine, and the route along the canal is open, but our ride leader has hurt her wrist and nobody else knows the way properly. So we've had to cancel and go next weekend instead. Sorry about that.

questions.prompt: Listen to the announcement. How much do club members normally pay for the workshop?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Woman] Attention, please. Places on next month's bicycle maintenance workshop are now open. The standard price is thirty pounds, but club members pay only eighteen. Anyone who books before Friday saves a further two pounds. Numbers are limited, so please sign up at the desk in the foyer.

questions.prompt: Listen to the conversation. What will the two friends do first?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Man] Shall we ride straight up the hill? [Woman] The café closes at four, and it's only two now, so there's no rush. [Man] True. We could stop for a drink first. [Woman] Actually, my back tyre feels rather soft. Why don't we pump it up at the bike shop, and decide afterwards? [Man] Good idea. Let's do that.

questions.prompt: Listen to the message. How does the speaker suggest getting to the festival?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Man] Hi, it's about Saturday's cycle festival down at the docks. I'd avoid driving if I were you — the car park is closed for building work. You can take a bicycle on the train, but not before ten in the morning, and we need to be there by nine. Honestly, the simplest thing is to ride along the canal path. It only takes twenty minutes from the centre.

questions.prompt: Listen to the announcement. What does the speaker recommend that riders do today?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Woman] Riders, please listen carefully. Because of the marathon, the riverside road will be closed from ten until two this afternoon. The bridge stays open, although you'll have to get off and walk your bicycle across it. The route through the park isn't affected at all, so that's the one we'd recommend today.

choices: use the route through the park

questions.prompt: Listen to the voicemail. What does the caller want Hugo to do?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Woman] Hi Hugo, it's Selina from the club committee. I've read the draft newsletter — it's very well written, and the photographs are excellent, so no changes needed there. There is one thing, though. The date of the summer ride is wrong. It says the fourteenth, but we agreed on the twenty-first. Could you correct that before it goes out on Friday? Thanks.

choices: correct the date of the ride

questions.prompt: Listen to the conversation about a new cycle lane. How does the woman feel about it?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Man] Have you tried the new cycle lane on Bridge Street? [Woman] I rode along it on Monday. It's certainly wide, I'll give them that. [Man] So you'll use it every day, then? [Woman] I'm not so sure. It stops suddenly at the roundabout, and delivery vans park in it all morning. [Man] Fair point.

choices: She is enthusiastic and will use it daily
She thinks the lane is far too narrow
She believes it is a complete waste of money
She has serious doubts despite one good feature

questions.prompt: Listen to the announcement. What should riders do in Market Square?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Man] Attention, cyclists. The tunnel under the station is closed this evening for emergency repairs, and it will not reopen tonight. Riders heading north should follow the signed diversion through Market Square and rejoin the route at the canal bridge. Please walk your bicycles through the square itself, as it is very busy with pedestrians at this hour.

choices: wait for the tunnel to reopen
turn back and use the canal bridge

questions.prompt: Listen to the conversation. Why has the club meeting been moved to the park café?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Woman] Are we still holding the club meeting in the hall on Thursday? [Man] We've moved it to the park café, actually. [Woman] Is that because the hall's being decorated? [Man] Partly, but mainly because far more people said they'd come along if we met somewhere they could leave their bikes safely outside. [Woman] That makes sense.

choices: The café charges nothing for the room
Members wanted somewhere to leave their bicycles
The hall is too small for the group

questions.prompt: Four people talk about why they cycle. Match each statement to the correct speaker.
questions.passage:
TRANSCRIPT: Speaker A: I started riding to work the week the bus fares went up again. That was the only reason, really — I couldn't keep paying that much just to sit in traffic. Two years on, the bike has paid for itself several times over, and I've stopped thinking about the cost of getting anywhere.
Speaker B: For me it's a question of speed, pure and simple. The bus takes forty minutes, the car takes thirty-five on a good day, and I do it in twenty-two whatever the traffic is doing. Nothing else in this city is that reliable. If something faster came along tomorrow, I'd probably switch.
Speaker C: I ride because of what it does to my head, not my legs. Ten minutes on the bike after a difficult day and whatever I was worrying about has somehow shrunk. I'm no fitter than I was, I suspect, but I arrive home calm instead of furious, and that's worth a great deal.
Speaker D: I'll be honest, the main roads frighten me. Buses come past far too close, and I've had a couple of near misses. So I stick to the old railway path and the towpath by the canal, where there's nothing bigger than another bicycle. It means longer journeys, but I actually arrive relaxed.

statements: This person began cycling because public transport became too expensive.
This person rides because it is quicker than the alternatives.
This person keeps well away from busy roads.
This person values the effect that cycling has on their mood.

questions.prompt: Listen to two people discussing a proposal to make cycle helmets compulsory. Who expresses each opinion?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Man] Have you seen the proposal to make cycle helmets compulsory for everyone? [Woman] I have. And before you ask — yes, I always wear one. I've seen what a fall on to a kerb can do, and a helmet clearly takes a lot of the force out of a head injury. [Man] That's exactly my point. If it protects you, it should protect everybody. Make it the law. [Woman] That's where I hesitate. In the cities that have introduced a helmet law, the number of people riding at all has dropped sharply — by a quarter in some cases. Fewer riders means drivers grow less used to seeing bicycles, and the roads end up more dangerous, not less. [Man] I take the point, but I'd still fine anyone who ignored the rule. Otherwise the law means nothing at all. [Woman] I wouldn't go that far. Punishing someone who can't afford a thirty-pound helmet doesn't strike me as fair. [Man] Fair enough. Where I do agree with you completely is that none of this touches the real problem. A helmet does absolutely nothing about a lorry turning across a cycle lane. [Woman] Precisely. Build the lanes properly, redesign the junctions, and you prevent the crash instead of softening it. That has to come first. [Man] Agreed. Design first, headgear second.

statements: Wearing a helmet reduces the risk of a head injury.
A compulsory law would lead to fewer people cycling.
Riders who ignore the rule should be fined.
Safer road design should be the main priority.

questions.prompt: Listen to the extract from a talk about traffic and air quality, and answer both questions.
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Female speaker] Ask most people whether it is healthier to cycle through the city or to drive through it, and they hesitate. Surely the cyclist, breathing hard beside a queue of exhaust pipes, takes in far more dirty air than the driver sealed inside a car? It is an intuition almost everybody shares, and the measurements simply do not support it. When researchers fit air-quality monitors to drivers and to riders on the same route, they find that concentrations of the worst pollutants are consistently higher inside the vehicle. The cabin traps the fumes of the car in front, and the ventilation system obligingly pumps them in. The cyclist, a metre to the side and slightly above the queue, is often in noticeably cleaner air. Now, I am not claiming that riding in heavy traffic is pleasant, or that we should stop worrying about roadside pollution. My point is about how we judge risk. We trust what we can see. The rider looks exposed, the driver looks protected, and a sheet of glass persuades us of something the instruments flatly deny. Any campaign that publishes the figures and then expects behaviour to change has, I think, misunderstood the problem. Before people will act on the evidence, they must first be shown why what they feel is wrong.

questions.text: What is the speaker's main claim?
choices: Cycling in heavy traffic is completely safe for the lungs
Drivers often breathe worse air than the cyclists beside them
Roadside pollution has been exaggerated by researchers
Ventilation systems in cars are steadily improving

questions.text: What does the speaker imply about public health campaigns?
choices: They should target cyclists rather than drivers
They rarely receive enough funding to succeed
Presenting the evidence is not enough on its own
They ought to avoid mentioning pollution figures

questions.prompt: Listen to the extract from a talk about cycle lanes, and answer both questions.
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Male speaker] Every council that builds a cycle lane wants a number for its annual report, and the number it usually reaches for is the count of bicycles passing a sensor at eight in the morning. I want to suggest that this figure is close to useless. A lane can carry a great many riders and still be a failure, because a rush-hour count tells you nothing whatever about who is missing. Look carefully at those cyclists and a pattern emerges: they are overwhelmingly fit adults, in a hurry, wearing specialist equipment. These are the people who will ride whatever you build — through traffic, up hills, in the rain. Their presence measures their own determination, not the quality of your lane. The genuinely revealing questions are quite different ones. Are there twelve-year-olds riding to school on it, unaccompanied? Are there people in their seventies doing the shopping? Is anybody carrying a small child? Those riders are, if you like, the warning birds of the network: the first to disappear when a route begins to feel unsafe, and the last to come back. A city whose lanes are used only by the confident has not built a transport system at all. It has built a corridor for the brave — and then congratulated itself on the traffic count.

questions.text: What is the speaker's main point?
choices: The range of people using a lane matters more than the number of them
Councils should stop counting cyclists altogether
Fit adult commuters should be discouraged from using cycle lanes
Cycle lanes are usually built in the wrong districts

questions.text: What does the speaker imply about lanes used mainly by fast, confident riders?
choices: They are carrying more traffic than the council expected
They still feel too dangerous for most other people
They will very soon need to be widened
They show that the council's investment has succeeded
